data.title: Key facts: FAA certifies Boeing 737 MAX 7 and 10; RBC predicts 520 deliveries in 2026
data.description:
• FAA chief Bryan Bedford confirmed the agency is not blocking Boeing's 737 MAX 7 and MAX 10 certification, emphasizing that Boeing must complete the required work for approval.1
  • RBC forecasts Boeing will deliver 520 737 MAX planes in 2026, reaching a production rate of about 47 per month by year-end, while remaining cautious on delivery growth.2

data.title: Key facts: Exxon Mobil shares rise 7.1%; oil exports drop 24%; top polluter
data.description:
• Exxon Mobil shares rose 7.1% in 2026, with earnings expected to increase 1% this year and over 20% in 2027. The current dividend yield is 3.2%.1
  • ExxonMobil (XOM) holds a stake in the Caspian Pipeline Consortium, which saw a 24% drop in oil exports in December due to maintenance and drone attacks. Kazakhstan's crude output fell 35% in January.2
  • Exxon Mobil was the top investor-owned polluter in 2024, responsible for half of global carbon emissions, prompting heightened scrutiny and calls for accountability in climate action.3

data.title: Key facts: Evercore ISI boosts Apple outlook; strong iPhone sales expected; Siri revamp planned
data.description:
• Evercore ISI added Apple (AAPL) to its 'Tactical Outperform' list, forecasting mid-to-high single-digit revenue growth and double-digit EPS growth, with a price target of $330.1
  • Apple's stock is gaining investor interest after a dip, with strong iPhone 17 sales expected to boost fiscal Q1 results. UBS predicts 12-13% growth, estimating 84.5-85 million units sold.2
  • Apple is set to revamp Siri, transforming it into a built-in chatbot for iPhone and Mac devices, in response to competition from OpenAI.3

data.title: Key facts: Delaware Life adds Bitcoin to annuities; BlackRock at forum; Fink's dinner disrupted
data.description:
• Delaware Life Insurance is adding Bitcoin-linked exposure to its retirement annuities through BlackRock's index, combining US stocks with a small allocation to Bitcoin via its iShares ETF.1
  • BlackRock is attending the London Digital Assets Forum with J.P. Morgan, Mastercard, and Stripe to discuss the UK's new Cryptoasset Reporting Framework, effective January 1.2
  • BlackRock CEO Larry Fink held a dinner at the World Economic Forum with key leaders. The event ended suddenly after U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's controversial speech faced heckling.3

data.title: Key facts: Crypto market rules spark tensions; Coinbase CEO discusses CLARITY; COIN declines 4.4%
data.description:
• Debate over crypto market rules is heightening tensions between banks and the crypto sector, impacting Coinbase Global's capacity to provide stablecoin rewards.1
  • Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong discussed progress on the federal crypto market structure bill, CLARITY, emphasizing collaboration for positive outcomes for stakeholders and the American public.2
  • Coinbase Global (COIN) experienced a decline of 4.4% on Tuesday in response to the U.S. announcement regarding potential tariffs on several European countries.3

data.title: Key facts: Citigroup's transformation timeline questioned; CEO doubts Trump plan
data.description:
• Analysts questioned Citigroup's transformation timeline, competitive stance, and net interest income, while management discussed investments and strategies to boost performance and efficiency.1
  • Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser expressed doubts about President Trump's plan to cap credit card interest rates, warning it could restrict credit access and negatively impact the U.S. economy.2

data.title: Key facts: Broadcom faces stock decline; semiconductor demand rises
data.description:
• Broadcom is expected to benefit from rising estimates in the semiconductor sector, driven by increased demand for compute and networking related to AI advancements.1
  • Broadcom's stock fell 4.9% and 5.4% due to a tech sector downturn, driven by rising geopolitical tensions and a shift to safer assets.23

data.title: Key facts: BigBear.ai delays annual report; CEO addresses share concerns
data.description:
• BigBear.ai delayed its annual report and restated 2022 financial results due to accounting errors, raising concerns about its reporting practices and causing a notable decline in stock price.1
  • BigBear.ai CEO Kevin McAleenan stresses the need to increase authorized shares due to low availability. He assures shareholders that this won't lead to immediate new share issuance.2

data.title: Key facts: Berkshire Hathaway to Resell Kraft Heinz Stake; CEO Eyes Acquisitions
data.description:
• Berkshire Hathaway plans to resell its 27.5% stake in Kraft Heinz, about 325.4 million shares worth $7.3 billion, following a 23% stock drop and upcoming company split.123
  • Berkshire Hathaway's new CEO, Greg Abel, manages $382 billion in cash. Analysts suggest boosting returns by acquiring more shares of quality businesses or using cash-secured put options.4

data.title: Key facts: B. Riley Raises Cytokinetics Price Target to $108; Stock Options Issued
data.description:
• B. Riley Securities has increased the price target for Cytokinetics to $108.00 per share, up from the previous target of $90.00.1
  • Cytokinetics issued stock options to new employees as part of Nasdaq Listing Rule 5635(c)(4), providing incentives for joining the company.2

data.title: Key facts: Anthropic's CEO highlights enterprise model stability; Isomorphic Labs delays trials to 2026
data.description:
• Anthropic's CEO Dario Amodei noted the stability of their enterprise model compared to the unpredictable consumer spending challenges faced by companies like Google in the AI sector.1
  • Isomorphic Labs, backed by Google, has delayed its clinical trials to late 2026, shifting from the previous target of late 2025, as stated by founder Demis Hassabis.2

data.title: Key facts: American Eagle Outfitters Q3 Revenues Rise 5.7%; Shares Up 22.9%
data.description:
• American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) reported Q3 revenues of $1.36 billion, up 5.7% year-on-year, exceeding forecasts. Aerie's strong sales drove AEO's stock up 22.9% to $25.70.1
  • American Eagle Outfitters' shares rose 1.5% following President Trump's tariff cancellation on Europe, amid ongoing Greenland deal talks with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte.2

data.title: Key facts: Amazon CEO Jassy cites tariffs, AI demand; opens large store
data.description:
• Amazon CEO Andy Jassy noted that tariffs are raising consumer prices as third-party sellers deplete pre-tariff stock. Amazon's stock recently dropped about 3% on Tuesday.12
  • Amazon CEO Andy Jassy stated at the World Economic Forum that demand for AI computing resources is unprecedented, solidifying Amazon Web Services' status as the top global cloud provider.3
  • Amazon will open its largest retail store in Orland Park, Illinois, covering 230,000 square feet. It will sell groceries and general goods while serving as a fulfillment center.4

data.title: US Stocks Sharply Higher as Greenland Tensions Cool
data.description:
US stocks were sharply higher on Wednesday afternoon, recovering part of the previous session’s losses as markets welcomed President Trump’s remarks at the World Economic Forum ruling out the use of military force in efforts to acquire Greenland.

The S&P 500, Dow, and Nasdaq 100 rose over 1.6% as the easing of geopolitical risk prompted a rebound in equities and Treasuries after a sharp risk-off move earlier in the week.

Gains were led by technology shares, with Intel, Micron, and Intuit rising between 7% and 10%, while broader sectors also stabilized as fears of an abrupt escalation in US–Europe tensions receded.

However, Trump reiterated his intention to pursue control of Greenland and maintained the threat of economic pressure on European allies, keeping trade uncertainty elevated.

On the corporate front, Netflix fell about 3% after flagging higher content spending and costs tied to its Warner Bros.

Discovery deal, while Johnson & Johnson slipped despite issuing stronger guidance.

data.title: Treasury Markets React to Trump's Softer Tone About Greenland — Market Talk
data.description:
1451 ET - The 10-year Treasury yield falls and the two-year is little changed, while the dollar rises, after Trump used social media to call off his latest threat of tariffs on European countries. He says talks about Greenland are progressing and "I will not be imposing the Tariffs that were scheduled to go into effect on February 1st." The twist seems to calm markets. The WSJ Dollar Index rises, with the greenback up 0.4% against the euro and 0.2% versus the yen. It's up nearly 1% against the Swiss franc. The 10-year yield is at 4.257% and the two-year at 3.593%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1410 ET - Mexico's inflation likely accelerated in the first half of January as higher excise taxes and import tariffs went into effect. Analysts in a Wall Street Journal survey expect the consumer price index to have risen 0.41% in the first two weeks of the month, pushing the 12-month rate up to 3.87% from 3.66% in the second half of Decmber. Core CPI likely rose 0.50%, with the annual rate increasing to 4.54% from 4.31%. "Our base case assumes that producers of soft drinks and tobacco will pass through the full extent of the excise tax increases to consumers within the first two weeks of the year. In contrast, items subject to new tariffs are expected to reflect the import duties more gradually," analysts at Barclays say in a note.Statistics institute Inegi is scheduled to report mid-January inflation on Thursday. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1355 ET - The IMF executive board says Canada's major medium-term economic challenge remains boosting the country's productivity and competitiveness. In a release, following up a staff report last month, the executive board says it's crucial Canada revitalizes business dynamism, strengthen innovation incentives, and deepen internal-market integration by implementing reforms at the subregional level. Canada's economy has proven resilient amid the tariff turbulence. Nevertheless, the executive board "noted that recent trade shocks have accentuated longstanding weaknesses in productivity and competitiveness, while the near-term outlook is subdued and risks are tilted to the downside." The IMF board did offer kudos to PM Mark Carney's efforts to reorient government spending toward investments in infrastructure and resource development, and to curb spending on public-sector administration. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1304 ET - Financial market uncertainty about the U.S. acquiring Greenland amid opposition from other countries looks to be easing after President Trump rules out a military takeover in remarks at Davos, while signaling a willingness to broker a deal through negotiations. "While it is hard to judge how the rather quixotic dispute over Greenland's sovereignty and military defense will evolve, Trump's remarks could be interpreted as a step towards de-escalation," says Jonas Goltermann of Capital Economics in a note. Some feared that market volatility similar to what was seen around the "Liberation Day" slate of tariffs last year was incoming as the situation with Greenland evolves, but that level of choppiness has yet to materialize. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1122 ET - Despite U.K. inflation coming in higher than expected in December, it still represents an undershoot relative to the Bank of England's expectations, Barclays economists say in a note. Annual inflation was 3.4% in December, up from 3.2% in November and above the consensus expectation of 3.3%. But the BOE in November forecast 3.5% at the end of the year while the food inflation undershoot was more pronounced at 4.5%, versus 5.3% expected, they say. Policymakers could still be wary about cutting rates for now and a February cut is highly unlikely, the economists say. However, by March, a cautious cut will have been validated by this undershoot and a weakening labor market, they say. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1106 ET - The cost of insuring euro-denominated high-yield credit against default falls as Trump's speech at Davos calmed markets after he said he wouldn't use force to acquire Greenland. U.S.-Europe trade war concerns also eased after the U.S. president avoided talking about potential U.S. tariffs on some European nations during the speech, XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. The iTraxx Crossover index of euro high-yield credit default swaps falls to 246 basis points following the speech, from 254bps before the speech, LSEG data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1103 ET - Annual inflation in the U.K. is set to fall to the Bank of England's 2% target in April, even after an uptick to 3.4% in December, ING's James Smith says in a note. The rise from 3.2% in November was mainly due to food inflation that should now trend lower and volatile airfares prices, he says. There's nothing to prompt the BOE to cut rates at February's next meeting, as policymakers will want to see more progress on services inflation. But the inflation backdrop should look a lot better from April, when one-off rises like water bills last year fall out of yearly comparisons. If weak hiring and benign wage growth trend continues, the BOE could be comfortable cutting rates in March, Smith says. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1045 ET - U.K. government bonds, or gilts, look undervalued, offering an attractive buying opportunity, Mizuho's Evelyne Gomez-Liechti says in a note. The term premia on gilts, or the extra yield investors demand for buying long-dated bonds, appears too elevated, Gomez-Liechti says. Mizuho expects ten-year gilt yields to fall below 4.350%. Ten-year gilt yields last trade down 1.3 basis points at 4.448%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1039 ET - National Bank Capital Markets has taken aim at how Statistics Canada seasonally adjusts inflation measures, which economists Matthieu Arseneau and Alexandra Ducharme say can create frustrating inconsistencies. It comes after headline annual inflation for December came in well above the consensus forecast, but two key core inflation measures pointed to more contained price pressures. The pair note every seasonally adjusted CPI sub‑component grew at a slower pace than the overall index, "an unprecedented intellectual impossibility." The economists say that had Statistics Canada used a bottom‑up approach then seasonally adjusted monthly inflation would have been just 0.06%, instead of the official 0.3%. They want the data agency to stop seasonally adjusting the aggregate index separately and rather aggregate the subcomponents. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1037 ET - The European Union is looking to reform the World Trade Organization's "most favored nation" principle in which members treat each other as equal trade partners. "We need a frank discussion on the link between MFN status and reciprocity, taking into account members' actual levels of market openness, their commitment to fair competition and transparency, and their evolving weight in global trade," he wrote in the Financial Times. Lower market barriers must be earned through stronger, credible commitments to the principles of free and fair trade, he writes. His comments, which also ask for a reform of governance and decisionmaking at the WTO, come ahead of trade ministers meeting at Davos amid a debate on U.S. tariff threats against allies over the ownership of Greenland. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

1035 ET - Yields on U.K. government bonds decline marginally after Trump delivered a speech at Davos. The U.S. President reiterated his intention to acquire Greenland purely for security reasons but said he would not use force. Ten-year gilt yields edge lower to last trade at 4.450%, down 1.2 basis points on the day, from 4.460% prior to the speech, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1013 ET - Corporate bond spreads, especially on investment-grade credit, are "very tight," leaving little room for further gains, State Street Investment Management's Desmond Lawrence says. There are "good reasons for those spreads to be tight." Corporates are generally in good shape with generally strong balance sheets, and are priced for a very good earnings outcome, the senior investment strategist says. Corporate bonds are "still interesting" but given how tight spreads are State Street IM sees "probably better" opportunities in U.S. mortgage-backed securities and structured credit such as collateralized loan obligations (CLOs), Lawrence says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Plumas Bancorp rises after posting higher Q4 profit
data.description:
** Shares of community lender Plumas Bancorp rise 9.61% to $48.81

** PLBC on Wednesday posted a rise in Q4 profit, helped by stronger net interest income

** Q4 net interest income rose to $6.9 million, driven by loan portfolio growth

** Deposits increased by 32% to $1.8 billion during the quarter

** Reported Q4 net income of $1.58 per share vs $1.31 per share in the same period last year

data.title: Kyber Network Crystal to Launch 25th FairFlow Liquidity Mining Cycle
data.description:
Kyber Network Crystal is starting the 25th cycle of its FairFlow Liquidity Mining Program on January 21, with the mining period running until January 28. The program is available across Ethereum, BNB Chain, Arbitrum, and Base, offering a total of 24,000 KNC in rewards alongside additional incentives. Participation terms and pool details are provided by the protocol.

Refer to the official tweet by KNC:

Kyber Network
@KyberNetwork

FairFlow Liquidity Mining Program - 25th Cycle starts in 1 hour âš¡

Mining period from Jan 21 - Jan 28, available on Ethereum, BNB Chain, Arbitrum and Base with 24,000 $KNC and other rewards.

Explore details 👇 pic.twitter.com/MCPOG3Bpgz

Jan 21, 2026

KNC Info

Kyber Network is a multichain cryptocurrency liquidity center that aims to optimize and simplify the DeFi trading process by providing the user with convenient, reliable and cost-effective access to the DeFi market.

The main product of the project, KyberSwap, is a decentralized exchange and liquidity aggregator operating on the principle of providing the best exchange conditions in various blockchain networks. To help users make decisions and automate processes, KyberSwap offers tools and functionality. The platform currently operates on 11 blockchain networks, including Ethereum, Polygon, and Avalanche.

KyberSwap is an aggregator that provides access to over 20,000 tokens and $34 billion in TVL through over 60 decentralized exchanges across 11 networks. Through dynamic routing among various liquidity pools, the platform guarantees the best exchange rates using advanced smart contracts and arbitrage capabilities, while not charging additional aggregation fees. In addition, KyberSwap offers a Discover tool to identify potentially popular DeFi tokens based on an analysis of network activity and trading trends.

For liquidity providers, KyberSwap offers the Dynamic Market Maker (DMM) protocol, an enhanced version of the standard AMM. Based on market volatility, fees are dynamically adjusted to reflect current risks. Additionally, the protocol includes a “programmable price curve” — Amplification (AMP) function, which allows you to effectively simulate high liquidity even with small pool volumes, while providers can configure AMP parameters in accordance with the characteristics of the selected pair of tokens.

The token of the Kyber Network project, Kyber Network Crystal (KNC), is based on the ERC-20 standard and plays the role of a motivational tool in the Kyber ecosystem. KNC holders have the opportunity to delegate their tokens to KyberDAO to participate in voting and receive rewards.

data.title: U.S. Stocks Jump As Trump Cites Greenland Breakthrough — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. stocks are building on gains after President Trump posts on Truth Social that he "won't be imposing tariffs that were scheduled to go into effect on February 1," against European allies who were opposing a U.S. acquisition of Greenland. Trump cites "a very productive meeting that I have had with the Secretary General of NATO." Trump says the deal, if consummated, will be a great one for the U.S. The DJIA climbs 752 points, reversing most of Tuesday's losses, with most major indexes up around 1.5% or more.(patrick.sheridan@wsj.com)

data.title: New World Accelerates Asset Sales to Hit HK$27 Billion Target
data.description:
New World Development (NDVLY) has stepped up efforts to complete at least one asset sale by the end of June, as management pushes to meet a HK$27 billion sales target and possibly deliver positive cash flow for the fiscal year ending in June, according to people familiar with recent investor discussions. In meetings with some investors, the company indicated it is accelerating disposals to close what is seen as a multibillion-dollar gap toward that goal, with some estimates putting the shortfall at more than HK$8 billion. While no specific transaction has been identified publicly, the focus is said to be shifting toward a core Hong Kong asset, as weaker investor appetite has weighed on attempts to sell other projects. The discussions remain preliminary and subject to change, and the company has not responded to requests for comment.

The renewed urgency comes against a backdrop of sustained balance-sheet pressure at the developer, which is controlled by Henry Cheng's family and was pushed close to default last year after years of debt-driven expansion. Although a large refinancing involving dozens of banks provided temporary relief, underlying challenges persist, including depressed property prices, a thin pipeline of new developments, and uncertainty surrounding parts of its retail portfolio, notably a major mall near Hong Kong's international airport. These headwinds have translated into heavy cash outflows, with HK$9 billion burned in fiscal 2025 and HK$23 billion the year before, after net debt climbed to 98% of shareholder equity at the end of June. In response, the company has suspended dividend payments on four outstanding notes since mid-2025, meaning shareholders will not receive distributions until bondholder payments resume.

To strengthen liquidity, New World has pursued a range of measures alongside asset sales. These include a bond swap that reduced debt by about $1.2 billion and a HK$3.95 billion loan secured against its Victoria Dockside complex. Potential divestment candidates have at various points included the Cheng family's Rosewood Hotel Group and mainland China projects such as K11 developments in Hangzhou, Shenzhen, and Shanghai, though progress has been slow due to valuation disagreements and protracted negotiations, including over the K11 Art Mall and the 11 Skies mall. Other funding plans, such as a proposed third-party capital injection and the sale of mainland Chinese toll roads by CTF Services, have stalled, leaving the December agreement to sell Australian power producer Alinta Energy to Sembcorp Industries for a $4.3 billion enterprise value as a notable exception in an otherwise difficult effort to stabilize the group's finances.

data.title: Multifamily Leaders Turn to F9Analytics as Antitrust Enforcement Accelerates Across Rental Markets
data.description:
Developed to replace legacy revenue management, F9Analytics "Profit Management" automation delivers multifamily operators maximum operational intelligence without regulatory risk.

NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / January 21, 2026 / With the recent Antitrust Judgment against legacy revenue management and the unlimited liability facing multifamily operators, it is not surprising why company executives are seeking to switch revenue management technology in 2026.

When legacy revenue management promised companies that their technology was safe, company executives took them at their word and defended against antitrust claims. In fact, companies disclosed such in their financial statements, stating "While company intends to vigorously defend against antitrust litigation, we are unable to predict the outcome or estimate the total amount of loss."

The problem facing companies today, the very software that was guaranteed safe, now subjects them to unlimited liability and reputational risk. Furthermore, given the "data prohibitions" placed on legacy revenue management under the recent US Antitrust Judgement, most if not all of the value to the multifamily operator (i.e. sharing real-time competitor data) has been rendered obsolete.

At F9Analytics, we don't have to make false promises because of software design flaws and poor analytics. On the contrary, the results of our technology promise best-in-class analytics to companies who seek optimal decision making with the same tractable models that buttress all the sciences. Unlike AI, we don't need to prove how well our models perform in mathematics, because our models are deterministic not probabilistic, and therefore logically identify optimal mathematical solutions without guessing or hallucination. For large financial institutions, deterministic models translate to decision making that is tractable, auditable, and compliant, offering predictable operating behavior and steadfast strategic governance.

In 2026, the strategic advantage will favor price automation technologies that offer multifamily operators the capability to maximize profitability, where marginal revenue equals marginal cost; thereby predicting, identifying, and ultimately pricing all inputs costs including incentive capital that drive operating performance.

The following table reflects this comparison:

Feature

F9Analytics (RealAccretive, Profit Management)

RealPage (AI Revenue Management)

Yardi (Revenue IQ)

Logic Engine

Deterministic Algorithms: Deterministic models analyze historical patterns by combining Federal Reserve economic data with private rent and expense data to predict and maximize unit and asset level profits portfolio wide.

AI / Machine Learning: Probabilistic models that analyze historical patterns and broad market data.

Hybrid / AI: Uses "Virtuoso" AI for automation but retains strong roots in enterprise accounting.

Data Source

Private Data Only: Data is ring-fenced and therefore uses owner's proprietary data "only" with public economic data from the Federal Reserve.

Aggregated / Public Data: Historically, used anonymized data from many landlords, though now restricted by 2025/2026 Judgements.

Mixed Data: Combines internal property data with publicly available competitor asking rents.

Legal Posture

Certified Compliant: US Certified Revenue Management Vendor, built specifically to remedy antitrust issues by avoiding the pitfalls of poorly designed legacy "price-fixing" models.

Settlement Restructuring: Under a DOJ consent decree, required to remove "coordinating functions" (i.e., real-time data sharing among competitors).

Lower Scrutiny: Faces less legal pressure than RealPage but defends against similar ongoing price-fixing claims.

Primary Strength

Profit Optimization: Superior to Revenue Management as proven in the Hospitality Industry, "Profit Management," maximizes a company's total operating efficiency, where marginal revenue equals marginal cost; the economic bedrock of financial performance.

Revenue Optimization: Maximizes income through automation and extensive market data intelligence.

Enterprise Control: Known as a "financial fortress" for deep accounting and complex portfolio management.

Learn More

To learn more about our operations research technology for real estate, please visit our website at https://www.f9analytics.com.

About F9Analytics

At F9Analytics, our vision to empower investors with superior pricing technologies that transform older operating paradigms into dynamic models for performance has taken form. Our commitment to research and development in real estate operations research has synthesized into the solutions we envisioned - ready to empower real estate companies with the necessary algorithms to leverage price to generate excess returns - be it in commercial or multi-family residential.

data.title: JET: Fastest-growing U.S. charter airline targets efficiency and growth with a flexible ACMI model
data.description:
The presentation highlighted rapid growth as a charter airline, leveraging certifications and a flexible ACMI model to expand fleet and profitability. Focus remains on operational efficiency, disciplined fleet management, and strong employee investment, with expectations for continued growth through 2026.

data.title: CBOT soybeans rise on a slow Brazil harvest launch
data.description:
Chicago Board of Trade soybean futures climbed on a slow start to the South American harvest as talks between U.S. and Chinese officials boosted export sentiment and shifted attention from a diplomatic dispute over Greenland.

  • CBOT March soybeans (SH26) settled up 11-1/2 cents at $10.64-1/2 cents per bushel.

  • CBOT March soymeal (SMH26) closed down $0.20 at $291.40 per short ton.

  • CBOT March soyoil (BOH26) rose 1.75 cent to 54.01 cents per pound.

  • Northern Brazilian showers are interrupting fieldwork at times, but serious slowdowns are not expected in the next weeks, according to forecaster Commodity Weather Group.

  • U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he had met with Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng, in what he called "positive" talks, adding that his Chinese counterpart had confirmed the completion of an initial U.S. soybean purchase commitment, which Washington has put at 12 million tons.

data.title: Microsoft, Other Software Stocks Report Earnings Soon. Here's How to Trade It. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jacob Sonenshine

Software stocks have been absolutely hated since autumn. Earnings offer a potential reprieve and an opportunity for traders.

The iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector exchange-traded fund is down 19% from a record high it reached in September. The pain is worst in the small to midsize names, with HubSpot down 42%, for example, and Zscaler down 30% in the same stretch. Even Microsoft is down 13% from a brief peak in September.

The problem is that the market is worried about long-term growth, especially for the hundreds of smaller firms that provide businesses with niche software products that could face competition from artificial intelligence.

There is certainly reason for concern. Products from companies such as OpenAI and Anthropic could eventually have business applications, which could make any given software company's business obsolete one day down the line.

The silver lining is that this sets up an opportunity to buy some beaten-down software stocks. At least for the near term, their results likely won't reflect lost market share or competitive threats, so earnings growth will probably turn out to be fine.

Larger profits mean these stocks may have nowhere to go but up, especially because they are cheaper now. While the stocks have dropped since September, analysts' estimates of aggregate sales and earnings for the companies in the software ETF are up, according to FactSet.

Now, the fund is trading for a bit less than 24 times the earnings its component companies are expected to produce over the next year, compared with just over 37 times in September. That is only about two points above the S&P 500's 21.9 times, the smallest premium in at least the past decade.

"The low expectations and low relative multiples could make for a good software trade in the first half of this year," writes Trivariate Research's Adam Parker.

Earnings season for software companies could trigger large gains.

Microsoft, which has earned more than expected in all of the past 13 quarters, reports its fourth-quarter 2025 results on Jan. 28. The company is positioned to weather competition, with a slice of OpenAI, a dominant position selling cost-effective AI solutions to companies, and potential 2025 free cash flow of more than $70 billion. Earnings growth could give investors a reason to buy.

Palantir Technologies, reporting Feb. 2, is another company to watch. A provider of products that help businesses identify find ways to save money and boost revenue, as well as tools for the military, it has turned in higher profits than expected in nine of the past 10 quarters.

Results from that pair could signal that earnings for the group are growing in line with expectations, or better, potentially lifting both their shares and other software stocks. Salesforce reports at the end of February and Oracle's numbers will land in early March.

The industry as a whole is growing briskly. Analysts expect aggregate sales for companies in the software ETF to increase by 15% a year for the coming two years. That should be enough to bring profit margins higher, sending earnings up 19% annually, FactSet data indicate.

Investors looking to exploit the potential opportunity should buy the ETF rather than one single stock. While the fund's price could get a boost during earnings season, it could also hold up well over the next couple of years.

The fund is heavily weighted toward the larger providers that are less likely to suffer from disruption to their businesses. And while OpenAI and Anthropic's Claude AI model do pose a competitive threat, investors are protected by buying the ETF. If either company does go public, they likely would find a place within the software fund.

After any earnings-driven gains, investors who do own smaller names could sell. Those are the ones most vulnerable to disruption and obsolescence in the long term.

"Pops will be used as liquidity events for a lot of different software names as the tape the last 6 months, conversations haven't indicated anything to the contrary...guilty until proven innocent right now," writes Jefferies trading analyst Jeff Favuzza.

In a nutshell, buy the big software ETF before earnings. Be ready to sell smaller individual names if they rally.

Write to Jacob Sonenshine at jacob.sonenshine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dow Jumps After Trump Calls Off New Tariffs on Europe — WSJ
data.description:
Stocks rallied after President Trump said that he had reached with NATO the framework of a deal over Greenland that would take away the need for new tariffs on Europe.

"Based upon a very productive meeting that I have had with the Secretary General of NATO, Mark Rutte, we have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region," Trump posted on Truth Social.

In afternoon trading, the Dow was up more than 600 points, or 1.5%.

Earlier, the president said that the U.S. wouldn't use force to acquire Greenland. Trump, speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, called for immediate negotiations over the U.S. taking control of the Danish territory. He talked up the successes of the U.S. economy and said Europe wasn't heading in the right direction.

European lawmakers, meanwhile, put on hold plans to slash tariffs on a range of U.S. imports. The move came in response to Trump's threat to put tariffs on some European countries over Greenland

Major U.S. stock gauges rose after Trump's comments, then pared those gains by midday. They had sold off Tuesday, with the Nasdaq composite notching its worst day since October. Gold prices extended this week's record-setting rally.

The Supreme Court heard arguments in the Trump administration's case to remove Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook. The justices expressed skepticism of the administration's arguments in a case will test the central bank's independence and the court's stance on regulatory power.

Earnings season continues, with results rolling in from Johnson & Johnson this morning. Netflix shares slid despite the streaming company posting strong earnings.

In other markets, Japan's long-term bond yields dropped from record highs hit Tuesday, a jump that spilled into global bond markets.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
